ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general battered fish product class; no specific beer-battered fish and chips composite entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers battered/breaded fried fish; no composite dish code for fish and chips exists. Chips component not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for beer-battered fish and chips as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of beer-battered fish and chips weighs approximately 450 g, typically comprising one or two battered fish fillets and a generous portion of deep-fried potato chips.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, battered or breaded, fried (composite estimate) and Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, oven-heated
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for beer-battered fish and chips as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for battered/breaded fried fish (FDC ID 175168) and deep-fried potato chips/fries (FDC ID 170720), weighted approximately 55% fish component and 45% chips component to reflect a typical 450 g plate composition. Input record values (energy 233 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 15.6 g, carbohydrate 13.3 g per 100 g) are broadly consistent with this composite and are retained as the primary per-100g basis, cross-referenced against USDA data. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not carry a direct entry for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — French fries / fried potato analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beer-battered fish and chips as a composite dish. GI estimated at 75 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: deep-fried potato chips/french fries, for which the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care) report GI values of 70–80 (mean ~75, high category). The wheat flour batter contributes additional refined carbohydrate but is a minor fraction of total carbohydrate. Composite GI weighted toward the potato chip component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 59.99) / 100 ≈ 44.9, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 13.33 × 4.5 = 59.99 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 266.7 mg/100 g; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, before accounting for tartar sauce and table salt additions.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g, request tartar sauce on the side, avoid additional table salt, and consider grilled fish alternatives to reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) driven by deep-fried potato chips; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 60 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~45, causing a significant postprandial glucose spike despite the moderating effect of co-ingested fat.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, restrict to a half-portion (approximately 200 g), pair with a non-starchy vegetable side, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the high glycemic load substantially challenges glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 15.6 g/100 g with saturated fat 3.33 g/100 g and trans fat 0.11 g/100 g; a 450 g serving provides approximately 70 g total fat, 15 g saturated fat, and 0.5 g trans fat, alongside 120 mg cholesterol, all of which contribute to atherogenic risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of deep-fried battered fish and chips; if consumed occasionally, limit to a small portion (under 150 g), choose heart-healthier cooking methods such as grilling or baking, and offset with high-fibre vegetables to mitigate lipid impact.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; the high refined carbohydrate and fat load further promotes a pro-inflammatory state.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations; request grilled or baked white fish without batter as a lower-AGE alternative that preserves the beneficial lean protein content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beer-Battered Fish and Chips is a classic Western dish, originating in the United Kingdom and widely served across Singapore's Western food stalls, hawker centres, and casual dining restaurants. The dish consists of white fish fillets — commonly dory, cod, or hoki — coated in a light, crispy batter made from wheat flour, beer, and seasoning, then deep-fried in hot oil until golden and crunchy. The beer in the batter produces carbon dioxide bubbles that create an airy, crisp texture when fried. The fish is served alongside a generous portion of deep-fried potato chips (thick-cut fries), typically accompanied by tartar sauce and a lemon wedge. The flavour profile is savoury and rich, with a crispy exterior giving way to moist, flaky fish inside, contrasted by the soft interior of the chips. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 233 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (10 g/100 g), high total fat (15.6 g/100 g) from deep-frying, and moderate carbohydrate (13.3 g/100 g) primarily from the batter and potato chips. Sodium is elevated at 267 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is high (~75) due to the deep-fried potato component, making this dish a significant glycaemic challenge. It is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. The dish contains fish and gluten allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified preparation without specific sourcing confirmation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beer-battered fish and chips?

Is fish and chips suitable for hypertension?
What are healthier alternatives to fish and chips?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for beer-battered fish and chips as a composite dish in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy component entries for battered fried fish and deep-fried potato chips, weighted by typical plate composition, and cross-referenced against the input record values which are broadly consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried potato chips. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: individual fatty acid fractions for this composite deep-fried dish are not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy adapted source entries (FDC ID 175168 and FDC ID 170720) used for this record, and no direct Singapore HPB Foodo entry exists for this dish as of 2026-04-16. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: same basis as monounsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid sub-fractions not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries consulted. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not available as a single reported value in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source; the input record did not supply this value and no approved source provides a reliable composite figure. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: micronutrient not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. vitamin_b6_mg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. vitamin_b12_mcg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. folate_mcg_dfe set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. phosphorus_mg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. magnesium_mg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. zinc_mg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. selenium_mcg set to -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries for this composite dish. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite deep-fried dish is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy adapted source entries consulted. All -1 values reflect genuine data unavailability in the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Singapore HPB Foodo) as of 2026-04-16; no approved source provides these micronutrient and fatty acid sub-fraction values for this specific composite dish. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.11 g/100 g from the input record; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with residual trans fats from commercial frying oils and is retained. The input record listed ethnic_tags including 'Burger' and 'Beef Dish' which are clearly erroneous for this dish and have been corrected. Halal status is marked false as beer is an ingredient in the batter; the dish is not halal-certified in standard preparation. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried potato chips as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected erroneous ethnic_tags ('Burger', 'Beef Dish') from input record.
Replaced forbidden grabfood data source with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy adapted composite as approved source.
Added mandatory multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish rules.
Populated full aliases and aliases_by_language with multilingual coverage.
Added GI value (75, high) with University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 analogous estimate basis and GL calculation.

Replaced input health_suitability (diabetes/hypertension/rheumatoid_arthritis) with RESULT template health_context structure including Type 2 Diabetes (machine key type2_diabetes), Hypertension, and Cardiovascular Disease as required conditions.
Updated ontology match_type fields to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2.
Expanded notes to individually document every -1 field with field-specific reason and source consulted, resolving g3_numeric_hygiene gate failure from iteration 2.
Set halal flag to false due to beer ingredient.
Verified aliases consistency: all aliases_by_language strings present verbatim in flat aliases array.
